evals.name: remote-work-blog
evals.prompt:
I dictated this blog post about remote work by talking into my phone. Can you tidy it up and turn it into a proper blog post? It's pretty rough since I was just talking. Here's the transcript:

so um okay so I want to write about like remote work because I've been doing it for a while now and I think I think the thing that people don't get about remote work is that it's not really about where you work right it's about how you work and um so so what happened was I started working from home like three years ago and honestly at first I was like this is great I love this I can you know I can work in my pajamas I don't have to commute and I was like why doesn't everyone do this

but then after a while I realized that I was actually I was you know I was less productive not more and I think the reason was that I didn't have you know I didn't have like a dedicated space for work like I was working on the couch or in bed or wherever and so my brain didn't really you know it didn't really get into work mode um so the first thing I'd say to anyone starting remote work is get a dedicated space like it doesn't have to be a whole office but just a desk that's just for work

and the other thing is um you really need to you know to set boundaries because when you work from home it's really easy to just keep working because your work is always there right it's always right there in your house and so I found myself like checking emails at 9pm and you know doing work stuff on weekends and that's not that's not good that's a really bad habit so so my advice would be to like set actual work hours and stick to them like treat it like a real job because it is a real job

um I don't know I think remote work can be really great if you do it right but you have to be you have to be intentional about it you can't just like fall into it and expect it to work because it won't it takes practice and it takes discipline but once you figure it out it's like the best thing ever honestly
evals.expected_output: A coherent blog post about remote work, with the author's voice and ideas preserved, filler removed, false starts collapsed, broken into paragraphs, with a suggested title.

evals.name: technical-post
evals.prompt:
I recorded myself talking through this blog post about learning to code. It's transcribed pretty messily with lots of ums and stuff. Can you clean it up into a readable post? Keep my technical terms right please — I work with these tools. Here it is:

okay so um I want to talk about like my experience learning to code because I get asked this a lot and I think people people have this idea that like you need to be you know a math genius or something to learn to code and that's just not true um so I started learning to code I don't know maybe two years ago and I started with Python because everyone said Python was easy and um honestly it was it was pretty easy to pick up the basics like variables and loops and functions and stuff but then when I got to like object-oriented programming I really struggled I just I couldn't I couldn't get it for a while

and I think the reason was that um I was trying to learn it from like a textbook and you know reading about classes and objects in the abstract it just didn't make sense to me it was like I was reading a foreign language um so what finally worked for me was I started actually building stuff like real stuff not just you know not just the exercises in the book but actual projects and once I started doing that it started to click because I could see like oh this is why you would use a class here like here's a real problem and here's how this thing helps solve it

so my advice for anyone learning to code would be don't just read don't just do tutorials like actually build something from the start it doesn't have to be anything big like my first real project was just a script that you know that organized my downloads folder but it was real it was something I actually used and that made all the difference

um the other thing I'd say is like don't get stuck on one language like I started with Python but then I needed to use JavaScript for something and I was scared to switch but it turns out once you know one language learning another is way easier because the concepts are the same it's just the syntax is different so don't worry too much about picking the right first language just pick one and start and you can always switch later
evals.expected_output: A technical blog post about learning to code, with Python, JavaScript, and programming concepts (classes, objects, object-oriented programming, variables, loops, functions) preserved correctly. Speech artifacts removed, paragraphs added.

evals.name: personal-story
evals.prompt:
Here's a voice memo I recorded about something that happened to me. It's emotional so I was rambling a bit. Can you turn this into a blog post? I want to keep my actual words as much as possible — this is my story in my voice:

so um I don't even know where to start with this but um last month my dog Benny died and it was it was really hard like really really hard and I know some people might think it's silly to be this upset about a dog but he was my best friend for fourteen years fourteen years you know like that's longer than most of my friendships longer than my marriage even and um

it happened really suddenly like he was fine one day and then the next day he wasn't and we took him to the vet and they said it was cancer and it was you know it was too late there was nothing they could do and I was just I was in shock I couldn't I couldn't process it and the vet was so kind she she let me stay with him and hold him while he went and I'm grateful for that I'm really really grateful for that because at least I got to say goodbye

and um the thing I want to say about this is like grief is weird like it doesn't it doesn't follow a schedule you know people tell you like oh it gets easier after a week or a month or whatever but for me it's been a month and some days are fine and some days I just I just cry for no reason like I'll be at the grocery store and I'll see dog food and I'll just lose it and that's that's okay that's normal I think

so I guess what I want to say to anyone going through this is like don't don't let anyone tell you how to grieve don't let anyone tell you it's just a dog because it's not it's not just a dog it's your family it's your best friend and you're allowed to be sad and you're allowed to take as long as you need and Benny I miss you buddy I really really miss you
evals.expected_output: A personal blog post about the loss of a pet, with emotional emphasis preserved (like 'really really hard', 'really really grateful', 'I really really miss you'), emotional repetition kept, speech artifacts removed, paragraphs added, suggested title.
